sources: California Office of the Attorney General

top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: Conflict dominates the day. Russia hit Ukraine with another large overnight barrage — Kyiv's Air Force counted 229 drones, of which it says 212 were downed or jammed; 5 people were killed and 37 injured across the country, mostly in Dnipropetrovsk — while Ukraine pressed its deep-strike campaign, setting the Saratov oil refinery and a Rostov fuel depot ablaze. In south Lebanon, Israel seized the strategically perched Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh and ordered mass displacement south of the Zahrani River, drawing a French call for an emergency UN Security Council meeting. In Singapore, the Shangri-La Dialogue closed on a calmer note, with Defence Minister Chan Chun Sing urging 'coalitions of the able and willing' as both the US and China softened their tone.

stories.headline: Russia launches fresh overnight barrage on Ukraine; Kyiv sets Saratov oil refinery ablaze
stories.summary: Ukraine's Air Force said Russia launched 229 drones overnight into May 31, of which 212 were shot down or jammed, with impacts at 11 locations; authorities reported 5 people killed and 37 injured across Ukraine over the past day, most in the Dnipropetrovsk region. Ukraine's General Staff said its own forces struck the Saratov oil refinery and a fuel-storage depot in Russia's Rostov region, which caught fire, as part of Kyiv's deep-strike campaign against Russian energy infrastructure. Russia's defence ministry said it intercepted 216 Ukrainian drones over 10 regions. (Ukrainian and Russian military figures; battlefield damage not independently verified.)

stories.headline: Israel seizes Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh and orders mass displacement in south Lebanon
stories.summary: Israeli forces captured the strategic 12th-century Beaufort Castle on a hilltop near Nabatieh, Lebanon's fifth-largest city, and issued more than 10 displacement orders within 24 hours, telling all residents south of the Zahrani River to move north immediately. The advance follows Israel's crossing of the Litani River — its deepest push into south Lebanon since 2006. Israel said one soldier was killed in a Hezbollah drone strike a day earlier, bringing its toll to 25 since fighting escalated on March 2; Hezbollah said it kept firing rockets and drones at advancing forces. Lebanon's PM Salam accused Israel of a 'scorched-earth policy', and France requested an emergency UN Security Council meeting. (Israeli advance; Lebanese-reported impact.)

stories.headline: Shangri-La Dialogue closes in Singapore with US and China both striking a softer tone
stories.summary: The 23rd IISS Shangri-La Dialogue wrapped up in Singapore after three days. In a closing plenary on 'Evolving Security Partnerships in a Fragmenting World', Defence Minister Chan Chun Sing said countries should not be 'held hostage by the lowest common denominator' and should build flexible 'coalitions of the able and willing'. The forum ended more calmly than in recent years, with China's delegation toning down its rhetoric and the US striking a milder line on Beijing — a shift widely tied to the recent Xi–Trump summit.

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: It's the closing day of the Shangri-La Dialogue, which Singapore hosted (May 29–31), and it dominates the local agenda. Defence Minister Chan Chun Sing used his plenary to argue for flexible 'coalitions of the able and willing' as conflicts in Europe and the Middle East ripple through global supply chains, and the forum ended on a calmer note with both the US and China dialling down their rhetoric. Singapore also concluded the state visit of Vietnam's President To Lam, capping a week of agreements including a new Singapore–Vietnam Strategic Dialogue. Weather-wise, expect a cloudy, warm night and a partly cloudy, warm day tomorrow (high around 35°C) with a 60–90% chance of showers.

stories.headline: Chan Chun Sing calls for 'coalitions of the able and willing' as Shangri-La Dialogue closes
stories.summary: In the closing plenary on 'Evolving Security Partnerships in a Fragmenting World', Defence Minister Chan Chun Sing said multilateral institutions remain pillars of cooperation but countries cannot be 'held hostage by the lowest common denominator', urging flexible partnerships among like-minded states. He noted that conflicts in Eastern Europe and the Middle East are no longer geographically isolated and have disrupted global supply chains, and highlighted the Singapore-led, 17-country GUIDE initiative to protect undersea infrastructure launched on the forum's sidelines.

stories.headline: 23rd Shangri-La Dialogue ends in Singapore on a calmer note
stories.summary: Singapore concluded its hosting of the 23rd IISS Shangri-La Dialogue, which drew 44 countries, 54 ministerial-level delegates and more than 42 chief-of-defence-level officials over May 29–31. The forum closed more calmly than recent editions: China's delegation softened its rhetoric and the US struck a milder line on Beijing, while ministers from across the region — including Japan's defence minister, who spoke on the final day — addressed evolving security partnerships.

stories.headline: Singapore concludes Vietnam President To Lam's state visit
stories.summary: Singapore wrapped up the May 28–31 state visit of Vietnam's Communist Party General Secretary and President To Lam, who also delivered a keynote address at the Shangri-La Dialogue. During the visit, PM Lawrence Wong and President Lam marked the relationship with a tree-planting at the Singapore Botanic Gardens and witnessed several agreements, including the establishment of a Singapore–Vietnam Strategic Dialogue and MOUs on supply-chain resilience, agrifood and advanced manufacturing.
stories.source: Singapore Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MFA) / The Straits Times

stories.headline: Cloudy, warm night; partly cloudy and warm tomorrow with a high chance of showers
stories.summary: NEA's Meteorological Service 24-hour forecast (issued 5:40pm, May 31) is for cloudy conditions tonight and partly cloudy, warm weather tomorrow, with a 60–90% chance of rain. Temperatures are expected to range from a low near 26°C to a high around 35°C, with south-southwesterly winds of 10–20 km/h. Where thundery showers develop, there is an imminent lightning risk; readers should seek shelter if storms approach.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: All three theatres remain active. In Ukraine, the two sides kept trading strikes overnight into May 31 — Russia launched 229 drones (Kyiv says 212 were downed or jammed), killing 5 and injuring 37, while Ukraine set the Saratov oil refinery and a Rostov fuel depot ablaze in its deep-strike campaign. In south Lebanon, Israel escalated sharply, seizing Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh and ordering mass displacement south of the Zahrani River after crossing the Litani; France has sought an emergency UN Security Council meeting. In the Gulf, the picture is unchanged: a tentative 60-day ceasefire-extension framework still awaits Trump's approval, Iran disputes US claims about reopening the Strait of Hormuz, and the waterway stays effectively closed.

theatres.name: Russia-Ukraine War
theatres.status: Both sides trade strikes; Kyiv presses deep strikes on Russian oil refineries (Saratov, Rostov) while Russia keeps up drone-and-missile barrages

timeline.event: Ukraine says drone strikes killed at least 65 Russian drone-pilot cadets and an instructor at a training camp in occupied territory; Zelensky says a separate strike killed and wounded nearly 100. (Ukrainian claims; Russia had not confirmed tolls.)

Russia hits Kyiv with its largest attack on the capital of the war — Ukraine's Air Force says about 600 drones and 90 missiles were launched, including a hypersonic Oreshnik that struck Bila Tserkva; defences downed or jammed most. Kyiv officials report several killed and dozens injured. (Russia did not dispute the strike.)
A Russian drone crashes into an apartment building in Galati, eastern Romania, wounding two — Romania summons Moscow's ambassador and NATO/EU condemn the incursion, raising spillover concerns.

Ukraine strikes deep inside Russia overnight — a 'shadow fleet' oil tanker and depot at Taganrog, an oil facility at Armavir (~500km from the border), and refineries at Volgograd and Yaroslavl; Russian officials report two civilians injured at Taganrog. Russia fires about 90 drones and two ballistic missiles at Ukraine. Russia's Rosatom claims a drone hit a turbine hall at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant; Ukraine denies striking it, calling it 'propaganda'. (Both sides' accounts; nuclear-plant claim disputed.)

Russia launches another large overnight barrage — Ukraine's Air Force counts 229 drones, of which it says 212 are downed or jammed, with impacts at 11 locations; authorities report 5 killed and 37 injured across Ukraine, most in Dnipropetrovsk. Ukraine's General Staff says its forces struck the Saratov oil refinery and a Rostov fuel depot, both of which caught fire; Russia's defence ministry says it intercepted 216 Ukrainian drones over 10 regions. (Ukrainian and Russian military figures; latest state of play.)

theatres.name: US/Israel-Iran (Strait of Hormuz)
theatres.status: Ceasefire-extension framework unfinished; US threatens to resume strikes, Iran disputes US claims; strait still effectively closed

timeline.event: US and Israel launch a coordinated air war on Iran, striking military, nuclear and leadership sites; Iran retaliates with missiles and drones on Israel, US bases and Gulf states.
Iran declares the Strait of Hormuz 'closed' and threatens to attack ships transiting it; the IRGC boards merchant vessels and lays sea mines, choking the key oil chokepoint.
Trump says a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz is 'largely negotiated' and will be announced soon.

US and Iranian negotiators reach a tentative 60-day framework to extend the ceasefire, declare Hormuz shipping 'unrestricted' and require Iran to de-mine the strait within 30 days, and begin nuclear talks — but it awaits Trump's final approval. Iran disputes the characterisation, saying the waterway remains under its control; the strait stays effectively closed.

At the Shangri-La Dialogue, US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth says the US is 'more than capable' of resuming strikes on Iran if talks fail; an adviser to Iran's Supreme Leader accuses Trump of 'betraying diplomacy', underscoring that the framework is not finalised. The strait remains effectively closed. (Latest state of play.)

theatres.name: Israel-Lebanon / Gaza
theatres.status: Israel seizes Beaufort Castle and pushes toward Nabatieh, orders mass displacement south of the Zahrani River; France seeks an emergency UN Security Council meeting

timeline.event: Israel and Lebanon agree a US-brokered ceasefire (effective ~April 17), initially a short truce to halt fighting and open negotiations.

Netanyahu says he ordered the IDF to seize 70% of Gaza — beyond the truce terms — saying Israel already controls about 60%; the UN warns it would deepen the humanitarian crisis. In Lebanon, Israel strikes a southern Beirut suburb and southern towns. (Lebanese-reported tolls.)

Netanyahu says Israeli forces crossed the Litani River and seized commanding terrain in southern Lebanon — the deepest advance since 2006 — vowing to crush Hezbollah; Lebanese authorities report new deadly strikes. (Israeli claim; Lebanese-reported casualties.)

Israeli troops reach the outskirts of Nabatieh, fighting Hezbollah in nearby villages, as Israeli and Lebanese officers hold security talks at the Pentagon. Lebanon's army says an Israeli drone seriously wounded two of its soldiers near Nabatieh; one Israeli soldier is killed in a Hezbollah drone strike. (Israeli advance; Lebanese-reported casualties.)

Israel seizes the strategic hilltop Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh and issues more than 10 displacement orders within 24 hours, telling all residents south of the Zahrani River to move north. Israel's toll reaches 25 soldiers killed since March 2; Hezbollah keeps firing rockets and drones. Lebanon's PM Salam accuses Israel of a 'scorched-earth policy'; France requests an emergency UN Security Council meeting. (Israeli advance; Lebanese-reported impact. Latest state of play.)

outbreaks.label: Outbreaks
outbreaks.ai_summary: The DRC/Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic — a WHO-declared PHEIC since May 17 — is still spreading faster than responders can contain it. As of 28–29 May the DRC reported 125 confirmed cases (17 deaths) plus 906 suspected cases (223 suspected deaths) across Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu, while Uganda has 7–9 confirmed cases (agency tallies differ) and one death, and has closed a high-traffic border crossing. There is no licensed vaccine or specific treatment for the Bundibugyo strain. Separately, the MV Hondius Andes-hantavirus cluster holds steady at 13 cases and 3 deaths, with the ship disembarked in Rotterdam and no confirmed onward spread. Public risk outside affected settings remains low.

outbreaks.name: DRC / Uganda — Bundibugyo Ebola (PHEIC)
outbreaks.status: PHEIC since 17 May; UN warns spread is outpacing containment
outbreaks.scope_note: Lab-confirmed only, DRC + Uganda combined: DRC 125 confirmed / 17 confirmed deaths (DRC Ministry of Health, as of 28 May); Uganda 7 (WHO) to 9 (ECDC) confirmed, 1 death — agency tallies diverge, at least 3 Uganda cases imported from DRC. WHO/ECDC separately report 906 suspected cases / 223 suspected deaths in DRC as of 29 May. No licensed vaccine or specific therapeutic exists for the Bundibugyo strain.

outbreaks.name: MV Hondius — Andes hantavirus
outbreaks.status: Limited person-to-person spread aboard ship; off-ship risk low
outbreaks.scope_note: 13 cases = 11 lab-confirmed Andes virus (ANDV) + 2 probable; 3 deaths (WHO Disease Outbreak News, latest update late May 2026, corroborated by ECDC). All cases are MV Hondius passengers/crew from a single vessel; the '32' is the number of countries/territories tracing 600+ contacts, NOT countries with cases. The ship disembarked in Rotterdam on 18 May; its 23 crew entered quarantine there. No confirmed onward community transmission off the ship.

stories.headline: UN warns DRC/Uganda Ebola epidemic is spreading faster than containment
stories.summary: The UN and WHO warned that the Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic in eastern DRC is outpacing the response. As of 29 May, WHO/ECDC reported 906 suspected cases (223 suspected deaths) in the DRC alongside 125 confirmed cases and 17 confirmed deaths in Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu. Uganda has reported 7–9 confirmed cases (several imported from DRC) and one death. Insecurity in the affected provinces is hampering containment; WHO declared the epidemic a PHEIC on 17 May.
stories.source: UN News / WHO Disease Outbreak News / ECDC

stories.headline: Uganda closes a high-traffic border crossing as rare Ebola strain spreads
stories.summary: Uganda closed a high-traffic border crossing with the DRC to slow the spread of the rare Bundibugyo Ebola strain after a rise in cases, several linked to travel from the DRC, including two new infections among healthcare workers reported on 25 May. The Bundibugyo virus is uniquely challenging because, unlike Zaire ebolavirus, it has no licensed vaccine or approved specific therapeutic; care is supportive.

stories.headline: MV Hondius hantavirus cluster steady at 13 cases, 3 deaths; ship disembarked in Rotterdam
stories.summary: WHO's latest update puts the MV Hondius Andes-hantavirus cluster at 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths, all among passengers and crew of the single vessel. The ship reached Rotterdam on 18 May and its 23 crew entered quarantine; more than 600 contacts are being monitored across 32 countries, with no confirmed onward community transmission off the ship.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Edge and infrastructure flaws still lead this week. An actively-exploited authentication-bypass in Palo Alto's GlobalProtect VPN (CVE-2026-0257, in CISA's KEV) and a newly disclosed Linux kernel 'CIFSwitch' local-root bug both landed on May 30. A Gogs self-hosted Git zero-day (argument-injection RCE) is being exploited in the wild and was added to CISA's KEV, and a critical FortiClient EMS flaw (CVE-2026-35616, CVSS 9.1) is being abused to push an 'EKZ' infostealer disguised as a Fortinet patch. On the breach front, ShinyHunters leaked 4.9 million Charter Communications customer records, Dutch police dismantled a 17-million-device residential-proxy botnet, and California's attorney-general sued 23andMe's new owner over its 2023 genetic-data breach.

stories.headline: Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth-bypass (CVE-2026-0257) now exploited in attacks
stories.summary: Palo Alto Networks confirmed active exploitation of an authentication-bypass in PAN-OS GlobalProtect that lets attackers forge override cookies to log in as the local admin; exploitation was first observed on May 18 and a second wave on May 21. CISA added it to the KEV catalog. Rapid7 urges treating it as critical despite the 7.8 score. Affected configs use GlobalProtect with authentication-override cookies enabled.

stories.headline: New 'CIFSwitch' Linux kernel flaw gives local root on multiple distributions
stories.summary: Researchers disclosed CIFSwitch, a local-privilege-escalation bug in the Linux kernel's CIFS subsystem that fails to verify the origin of cifs.spnego key requests, letting an unprivileged user forge one and load a malicious NSS module to gain root. Present since 2007, it affects stock builds of distributions including Linux Mint, CentOS Stream 9, Rocky/AlmaLinux 9 and SLES 15; a kernel patch validates request origins.

stories.headline: ShinyHunters leak 4.9 million Charter Communications customer records
stories.summary: The ShinyHunters extortion gang leaked data on 4.9 million Charter Communications accounts after an April vishing attack compromised an employee's Microsoft Entra account and the firm's Salesforce instance. Exposed data includes names, emails, addresses, phone numbers and support-ticket details; Charter says no sensitive personal information or CPNI was taken.

stories.headline: Dutch police dismantle 17-million-device 'Asocks' residential-proxy botnet
stories.summary: The Hague police cybercrime unit and the Dutch NCSC took down the Asocks botnet — at least 17 million compromised consumer devices worldwide run via about 200 Netherlands-based servers — which had been sold as a 'residential proxy' service to route malicious traffic and launch attacks. The servers were seized and the botnet taken offline.

stories.headline: Gogs self-hosted Git zero-day exploited for remote code execution; added to CISA KEV
stories.summary: A zero-day argument-injection flaw in the Gogs self-hosted Git service lets an authenticated, non-admin user gain remote code execution on internet-facing instances — a crafted pull-request branch name (e.g. '--exec=...') is passed unsanitised to a git rebase command. It affects current releases (0.14.2 and 0.15.0+dev); Rapid7 reported it on March 17 but no fix had shipped as of the May 28 disclosure, and CISA added it to the KEV catalog after observing in-the-wild exploitation.

stories.headline: Critical FortiClient EMS flaw (CVE-2026-35616) abused to drop 'EKZ' infostealer disguised as a Fortinet patch
stories.summary: Arctic Wolf reported a campaign abusing the critical FortiClient EMS pre-auth access-control flaw (CVE-2026-35616) — already in CISA's KEV since April — to deploy an undocumented credential stealer dubbed 'EKZ'. The malware is disguised as a legitimate Fortinet endpoint patch and steals browser credentials, cookies and payment data from Chromium and Firefox. Fortinet shipped emergency hotfixes; Shadowserver reported roughly 2,000 internet-exposed instances.
stories.source: BleepingComputer / The Hacker News / Arctic Wolf
